exercise.label: ula-8-2-spansort
exercise.title: Does these vectors span the vector space?

statement.p: Determine whether each set spans the vector space or not.

match.premise: The set \{1,x,x^2\} in the vector space \mathbb{P}_2.
The set \{x,x^2\} in the vector space \mathbb{P}_2.

statement.p: Let V be a vector space and suppose \{\uvec_1,\ldots,\uvec_k\} is a set of vectors in V. Show that \zerovec is in \mathrm{span}\{\uvec_1,\ldots,\uvec_k\}.

li.p:
Write
A=\begin{bmatrix}1\amp3\\0\amp0\end{bmatrix}
as a linear combination of
\begin{bmatrix}1\amp 0\\0\amp 1\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}0\amp 1\\1\amp 0\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}1\amp 0\\1\amp 1\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}0\amp 1\\1\amp 1\end{bmatrix}.
Show that the above set of four matrices is a spanning set for \Mvec_{2,2}, the vector space of all 2\times2 matrices.

statement.p: Consider the vector space \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}} of infinite sequences of real numbers. A sequence a=(a_i)_{i\in\mathbb{N}} is called finitely supported if all but finitely many elements of the sequence are zero. In other words, a is finitely supported if there exists some N\in\mathbb{N} such that a_k=0 for all k\ge N.

solution.p:
Let S=\{e^0,e^1,e^2,\ldots\}. To prove the left-to-right implication, assume a\in\mathrm{span}(S). Then there exists some finite subset \{e^{k_1},e^{k_2},\ldots,e^{k_n}\} of S and scalars b_1,\ldots,b_n such that
a=b_1e^{k_1}+\cdots+b_ne^{k_n}.

Let N\in\mathbb{N} be a number that is greater than k_1,\ldots,k_n. Since each of the sequences e^{k_1},\ldots,e^{k_n} is zero after the first N elements, the same is true for their linear combination a. Thus, a is finitely supported.

To prove the right-to-left direction, assume a is finitely supported. Let N\in\mathbb{N} such that a_k=0 for all k\ge N. Then
a=(a_0,a_1,\ldots,a_{N-1},0,0,0,\ldots),
with infinitely many zeros following. Therefore,
a=a_0e^0+a_1e^1+\cdots+a_{N-1}e^{N-1},
and it follows that a\in\mathrm{span}(S).

statement.p: For each of the following sets of polynomials, determine whether the set is linearly independent. If it is linearly dependent, write one polynomial as a linear combination of the other polynomials in the set.
Determine whether each of the following sets of matrices is linearly independent. If it is linearly dependent, write one matrix as a linear combination of the other matrices in the set.

solution.p:
Let p_i(x) denote the ith of these polynomials. Suppose
C_1p_1(x)+\cdots+C_4p_4(x)=0.
Then collecting terms according to the exponent of x, we have
C_1a_1+C_2a_2+C_3a_3+C_4a_4=0, C_1b_1+C_2b_2+C_3b_3+C_4b_4=0, C_1c_1+C_2c_2+C_3c_3+C_4c_4=0, C_1d_1+C_2d_2+C_3d_3+C_4d_4=0.
The matrix of coefficients is just the transpose of the above matrix. There exists a non-trivial solution if and only if the determinant of this matrix equals 0.

statement.p:
Assume \uvec,\vvec,\wvec are linearly independent elements of some vector space V. Consider the set of vectors
R=\{2\uvec-\wvec,\;\wvec+\vvec,\;3\vvec+\tfrac12\uvec\}.
Determine whether R is linearly independent.

solution.p:
To determine whether R is linearly independent, we must solve the equation
a(2\uvec-\wvec)+b(\wvec+\vvec)+c(3\vvec+\tfrac12\uvec)=0.
If the only solution is the trivial solution, the set is linearly independent.

We rewrite the equation as follows:
(2a+\tfrac12c)\uvec+(b+3c)\vvec+(-a+b)\wvec=\zerovec.
Since \uvec,\vvec,\wvec are linearly independent, the coefficients in the last equation must all equal 0. In other words:
2a+\tfrac12c\amp =0, b+3c \amp =0, -a+b\amp =0.
We solve and find that the unique solution is a=b=c=0. Therefore, the set R is linearly independent.
